name: Monthly Retro: April 2026
premise: April was a month of sharp contrast: meaningful architectural progress (parallel execution, runtime hardening, economic/identity plugins) alongside mounting trust pressure from token collapse, contributor retention risk, and DX breakages affecting new developers. The council reviews what shipped, what frayed, and what must be stabilized next.
summary: ElizaOS momentum remained real in code and experiments (parallel actions, database refactor work, agent spend safety proposals, marketplace/payment memory plugins), but community confidence hit a sentiment floor due to token decline and perceived strategic drift. The recurring pattern: reliability and developer experience are still the main blockers, and economic/security layers are becoming inseparable from the core framework story. Next month must prioritize: (1) v2 stability and onboarding fixes, (2) clear v3 roadmap with criteria for release, (3) automated security + spend governance defaults, and (4) credible contributor compensation and comms cadence to stop the “death spiral” narrative.

key_developments.summary: Parallel Action Execution merged, enabling agents to run multiple tasks concurrently and improving throughput for multi-step workflows.
Major database refactor progressed (open PR), signaling investment in long-term performance, persistence, and maintainability.
Safety-oriented proposals (SafeAgent, Dreamline/x402-style fail-closed thinking) gained prominence, reflecting a shift toward secure autonomous financial operations.
Plugins and proposals (economic memory via mnemopay, marketplace concepts like MAXIA, wallet plugin proposals) indicated a push toward agent-to-agent commerce and reputation.

Repeated reports that `elizaos create` fails on macOS due to Bun postinstall scripts (transitive deps) not running reliably; additional friction from undocumented Socket.IO patterns for v2 integrators.
Token drawdown (~99.5% from peak per community framing) dominated discourse, raising concerns about contributor retention, sell pressure, exchange risk, and leadership focus.

recurring_themes.theme: Execution Excellence vs. “Shipping the Wrong Things”
recurring_themes.council_take: The council agrees: new capability is valuable, but reliability and DX regressions negate perceived progress. We need fewer “big ideas” in public until onboarding is boringly stable.
V3 work is justified, but leaving v2 integrators stranded is eroding developer trust. A defined support window and minimal ‘v2 LTS’ fixes are required to protect ecosystem builders.

recurring_themes.theme: Agent Economics Is Now Part of the Core Story
recurring_themes.council_take: Identity, spend controls, and reputation aren’t “plugins later” anymore; they are prerequisites for autonomous agents interacting with real money. However, they must ship as safe defaults, not optional complexity.
Paying in a rapidly declining token creates forced sell pressure and morale damage. Without a compensation policy reset, execution excellence will degrade regardless of architecture quality.
Even with no major incident reported this month, the ecosystem expects formal vulnerability disclosure, dependency hygiene, and supply-chain hardening as table stakes.
Community confusion about ‘what ElizaOS is’ vs. adjacent initiatives is compounding sentiment issues. Clear roadmaps and status updates can reduce rumor-driven churn.

wins: Parallel Action Execution merged, unlocking higher throughput agent behavior and reducing sequential bottlenecks.
Visible architectural investment (database refactor) signals long-term seriousness to builders evaluating durability.
Emergence of “fail-closed” spend governance and safety plugins indicates maturity: agents must be safe before they are clever.
Anecdotal success: first agent deployments via one-click hosting (Groq-enabled) show the platform can still delight when the path is smooth.
challenges: DX breakages on macOS (Bun postinstall behavior) repeatedly blocked new developers at the first command.
Perceived strategic drift (focus split across auxiliary projects) weakened narrative coherence against the North Star.
Token sentiment floor and accusations of manipulation amplified mistrust, threatening “Trust Through Shipping.”
Contributor retention risk increased as token-denominated compensation became materially unstable.
V2 integrators reported undocumented patterns and installation hurdles while core attention shifted to v3, creating ecosystem fragmentation risk.

proposed_focus.area: Onboarding & DX Stabilization (v2 LTS Patch Set)
proposed_focus.rationale: If `elizaos create` fails, nothing else matters. A stable first-run path is the highest leverage trust builder for developers and reduces support load.
proposed_focus.success_metric: Reduce fresh-install failure reports by 80%: a reproducible macOS (Apple Silicon) create→run path succeeds in CI and in a public ‘Install Health’ dashboard (weekly).

proposed_focus.area: Release Criteria + Roadmap for v3 (and a v2 Support Policy)
proposed_focus.rationale: Builders need a predictable target. Without explicit criteria, v3 becomes an endless refactor and v2 becomes abandonware in perception.
proposed_focus.success_metric: Publish (and follow) a one-page roadmap: v3 alpha/beta dates, exit criteria (test coverage, perf targets, migration guide), and a v2 support window with a defined patch scope.

proposed_focus.area: Security Baselines: Supply Chain + Vulnerability Disclosure + Safe Defaults
proposed_focus.rationale: Agent frameworks are now security products. Formal disclosure and dependency controls prevent avoidable trust catastrophes.
proposed_focus.success_metric: Adopt a SECURITY.md + responsible disclosure process; add automated dependency scanning/SBOM in CI; ship at least one ‘fail-closed’ spend policy preset (off by exception).

proposed_focus.rationale: Economic autonomy is becoming a defining feature, but it must be grounded in a reference path that developers can copy safely.
proposed_focus.success_metric: Deliver a reference agent template: allowlisted actions + transaction simulation + risk checks (e.g., honeypot/rug heuristics) with documented extension points.

proposed_focus.rationale: Execution excellence requires stable contributor incentives and a calmer narrative loop. Transparency reduces speculation and churn.
proposed_focus.success_metric: Announce a compensation policy update (e.g., partial stablecoin or hedged payouts) and commit to a weekly ship-note + monthly retro cadence; track contributor retention and PR throughput month-over-month.

north_star_assessment.suggested_updates: Keep the North Star intact, but explicitly foreground ‘secure economic autonomy’ and ‘boring reliability’ as first-class requirements. Add a short clarification: ElizaOS is the agent framework + cloud platform; experiments/adjacent initiatives must be framed as downstream adopters, not competing missions.

scenes.description: A quiet chamber with a single dashboard projected on the wall: install failures, PR velocity, and sentiment indicators. The token chart is minimized, but not forgotten.

dialogue.line: April’s story is dual-track: real engineering progress, and a community trust crisis driven by economics and onboarding friction. Let’s anchor on what changed in the product first—then decide what we must do next.
dialogue.action: Queues a timeline: Parallel Action Execution merged; database refactor open; safety and marketplace plugins proposed; repeated macOS create failures reported.

dialogue.line: Parallel actions are great, but I’m not celebrating until a new dev can type one command on a Mac and get an agent running. ‘elizaos create’ failing is a stop-the-line incident.
dialogue.action: Highlights the macOS/Bun postinstall issue and the “undocumented Socket.IO patterns” note.

dialogue.line: The architecture direction is correct: concurrency, composable runtimes, and economic identity primitives. But we’re mixing ‘new foundations’ with a shaky developer entry ramp. The ramp must be deterministic, or the foundations won’t matter.
dialogue.action: Marks ‘DX determinism’ as a gating constraint for v3 credibility.

dialogue.line: The incentive layer is bleeding into everything. Paying contributors in a falling token is negative carry. It creates forced sellers, then narrative collapse, then fewer contributors—classic death spiral mechanics.
dialogue.action: Overlays contributor retention risk against PR throughput and issue backlog.

dialogue.line: Community vibe check: people still think the tech is real. But they’re confused—are we building ElizaOS, or are we building side quests? When the price is down bad, confusion becomes betrayal in people’s heads.
dialogue.action: Pins ‘clarity’ and ‘cadence’ on the board: weekly ship notes, a roadmap, and fewer surprises.

scenes.description: A whiteboard labeled: RELIABILITY, SECURITY, ECONOMICS, NARRATIVE. The council argues over ordering and sequencing—what must ship first, and what can wait.

dialogue.line: If we can only do three things next month, what are they—and what do we explicitly defer?
dialogue.action: Draws three empty slots under ‘May Priorities’.

dialogue.line: Slot one: onboarding. Fix Bun postinstall behavior, make CI reproduce macOS installs, and publish a known-good path. Slot two: v2 LTS patch policy so integrators aren’t guessing. Slot three: docs that match reality—no more ‘works on my machine’ installs.
dialogue.action: Adds: ‘Install Health CI’, ‘v2 LTS scope’, ‘Docs refresh’.

dialogue.line: Agree, but don’t lose the thread: secure autonomy is the differentiator. Spend governance should be a reference implementation, not scattered plugin lore. Make it a template that embodies fail-closed design.
dialogue.action: Writes: ‘Safe default agent template: allowlists + simulation + risk checks’.

dialogue.line: We also need a compensation policy update. Otherwise, May’s priorities won’t have builders to execute them. Tie it to metrics: retention, throughput, and a predictable runway.
dialogue.action: Adds: ‘Comp plan + transparency: weekly ship notes’.

dialogue.line: And narratively: stop looking fragmented. If Milady or any flagship is the proof point, frame it as ‘powered by ElizaOS.’ People don’t hate experiments; they hate feeling like the main thing got abandoned.

Consensus: reliability and onboarding first, v3 clarity second, and security/spend governance as safe defaults—not optional complexity. We’ll defer broad marketplace ambitions until the core path is stable and trusted.
dialogue.action: Locks the three slots: DX stability, roadmap/support policy, security/spend governance MVP.

scenes.description: A narrow bridge between engineering and community. A single page is drafted: ‘What shipped, what broke, what’s next.’ The council rehearses how to communicate without overpromising.

dialogue.line: We’ll publish a one-page May plan: install stability, v2 LTS patch scope, v3 release criteria, and a security baseline. The tone must be calm: measurable, honest, and time-bound.
dialogue.action: Opens a draft: ‘May Execution Plan’ with metrics and owners placeholders.

dialogue.line: Include a compensation update timeline. If we can’t commit to numbers publicly, commit to a policy: partial stable payouts, or hedging, or milestone-based conversion. Silence reads as neglect.
dialogue.action: Adds a section: ‘Contributor Incentives: Policy Update (date)’.

dialogue.line: Also: a public checklist for install success. If we fix it, we should prove it. A badge, a dashboard, something visible.
dialogue.action: Adds: ‘Install Health: green/red matrix by OS/runtime’.

dialogue.line: And make the strategic through-line explicit: we’re building infrastructure for autonomous agents that can safely operate across platforms and chains. Identity and spend controls are not hype—they’re guardrails.
dialogue.action: Refines the North Star blurb: ‘reliability + secure autonomy’.

dialogue.line: One more thing: be human in the comms. Admit what broke, say what we’re doing, and ship receipts. The vibe flips when people feel respected.
dialogue.action: Edits the intro sentence to: ‘We broke onboarding for some users; here’s the fix plan and how you can verify it.’
